input.overview: Build a rich visual invoice template editor where users can choose from starter templates, customize fonts/colors/theme, and add or remove fields. The final template is saved per-org and applied automatically when generating PDFs.
input.plan:
# Invoice Visual Template Editor

## Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph db [Database]
    invoice_templates["invoice_templates table\n(id, org_id, name, config JSONB)"]
  end

  subgraph editor [Editor UI]
    TemplatePicker["Template Picker\n/dashboard/invoices/templates"]
    TemplateEditor["Visual Editor\n(live preview + controls)"]
    FieldPanel["Field Toggle Panel\n(show/hide fields)"]
    StylePanel["Style Panel\n(font, colors, layout)"]
  end

  subgraph render [Rendering]
    PDFPage["PDF Page\napp/.../pdf/page.tsx"]
    InvoicePreview["InvoicePreview component\n(renders from TemplateConfig)"]
  end

  TemplatePicker --> TemplateEditor
  StylePanel --> TemplateEditor
  FieldPanel --> TemplateEditor
  TemplateEditor -->|"POST /api/invoice-templates"| db
  PDFPage -->|"GET template config"| db
  db --> InvoicePreview
  TemplateEditor --> InvoicePreview
```

## Data Model

### New table: `invoice_templates`

```sql
CREATE TABLE invoice_templates (
  id         SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  org_id     INTEGER REFERENCES organizations(id),
  name       VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  is_default BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  config     JSONB NOT NULL,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

The `config` JSONB stores the full template definition:

```ts
interface TemplateConfig {
  templateId: "classic" | "modern" | "professional"  // base layout
  colors: {
    primary: string    // header background, accents
    secondary: string  // subheadings, borders
    text: string       // body text
    background: string // page background
  }
  fonts: {
    heading: string   // e.g. "Inter", "Playfair Display", "Roboto Mono"
    body: string
    size: "sm" | "md" | "lg"
  }
  fields: {
    // each field: show/hide + optional custom label
    logo: { show: boolean }
    senderPhone: { show: boolean; label: string }
    senderAddress: { show: boolean; label: string }
    clientPhone: { show: boolean }
    clientGstin: { show: boolean }
    hsnCode: { show: boolean; label: string }
    serviceDate: { show: boolean; label: string }
    cgstSgst: { show: boolean }
    terms: { show: boolean }
    bankDetails: { show: boolean }
    signature: { show: boolean }
    // custom extra fields (user-defined)
    custom: Array<{ id: string; label: string; value: string; show: boolean }>
  }
  lineItems: boolean  // false = single item (current), true = multi-row table
  pageSize: "A4" | "Letter"
}
```

## Files to Create / Modify

### New files

- `app/dashboard/invoices/templates/page.tsx` - template picker page (server component, loads saved config)
- `components/template-editor.tsx` - main editor shell (client, splits into preview + panels)
- `components/template-preview.tsx` - live A4 preview that re-renders instantly as config changes
- `components/template-style-panel.tsx` - color pickers, font selectors, layout options
- `components/template-field-panel.tsx` - toggle switches for each field + add custom field
- `app/api/invoice-templates/route.ts` - GET (load), POST/PUT (save), and list templates
- `lib/template-defaults.ts` - 3 hardcoded starter template configs (Classic, Modern, Professional)

### Modified files

- [`app/dashboard/invoices/[id]/pdf/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/invoices/[id]/pdf/page.tsx) - load org's active template config, pass to renderer
- [`components/invoice-view.tsx`](components/invoice-view.tsx) - load template and render preview in same style
- [`components/dashboard-layout.tsx`](components/dashboard-layout.tsx) - add "Templates" link under Invoices

## The 3 Starter Templates

| Template | Layout | Header Style | Accent Color |
|---|---|---|---|
| Classic | Traditional two-column | Gray header band | #1e3a5f (navy) |
| Modern | Clean, lots of whitespace | Left-aligned, no box | #6366f1 (indigo) |
| Professional | Bold full-width header | Color block with white text | #0f766e (teal) |

## How the Editor Works

1. User visits `/dashboard/invoices/templates`
2. Sees 3 template cards — clicks one to open the editor
3. Editor splits: **left = controls**, **right = live A4 preview**
4. Controls have 3 tabs: **Style** (colors/fonts), **Fields** (show/hide toggles), **Layout** (page size, line items)
5. Changes instantly update the preview via React state (no API calls yet)
6. "Save as Default" button calls `POST /api/invoice-templates` — stored as org's active template
7. All new PDFs rendered using this saved config automatically

## Multi-line Items (additive)

When `config.lineItems = true`:
- Add a `invoice_line_items` table: `(id, invoice_id, description, hsn_code, qty, rate, amount, cgst_rate, sgst_rate)`
- Invoice form grows a dynamic rows section
- PDF renderer loops over line items instead of the single `description` field

This is a schema change so it needs a migration, but is backward-compatible (existing invoices just have a single line item auto-created).

## Key Design Decisions

- Template config is pure JSON in the DB — no new rendering library needed (plain Tailwind + inline styles)
- Live preview uses the same `template-preview.tsx` component as the actual PDF — one source of truth
- Font choices are Google Fonts loaded via `next/font` or `@import` in a `<style>` tag (no extra packages)
- Color pickers use `<input type="color">` (native, no library)
- The feature is fully additive — existing invoices keep working unchanged until a template is saved

export const dynamic = "force-dynamic" import { notFound } from "next/navigation" import { sql } from "@/lib/db" import { numberToWords } from "@/lib/utils/number-to-words" import { PrintButton } from "./print-button" import { TemplatePreview, SAMPLE_PROFILE } from "@/components/template-preview" import { CLASSIC_TEMPLATE } from "@/lib/template-defaults" import type { TemplateConfig } from "@/lib/template-defaults" export default async function InvoicePdfPage({ params }: { params: Promise<{ id: string }> }) { const { id } = await params const invoices = await sql` SELECT i.*, json_build_object( 'id', c.id, 'name', c.name, 'email', c.email, 'address', c.address, 'gstin', c.gstin, 'phone', c.phone ) as client FROM invoices i LEFT JOIN clients c ON i.client_id = c.id WHERE i.id = ${id} ` const invoice = invoices[0] as Record & { client: Record } if (!invoice) notFound() // Load profile scoped to org const orgId = invoice.org_id as number const profiles = await sql`SELECT * FROM profiles WHERE org_id = ${orgId} LIMIT 1` const profile = profiles[0] as typeof SAMPLE_PROFILE | undefined // Load org's active template, fall back to Classic const templateRows = await sql` SELECT config FROM invoice_templates WHERE org_id = ${orgId} AND is_default = TRUE ORDER BY updated_at DESC LIMIT 1 ` const config: TemplateConfig = (templateRows[0]?.config as TemplateConfig) ?? CLASSIC_TEMPLATE // Load line items (if multi-item template) const lineItems = await sql` SELECT * FROM invoice_line_items WHERE invoice_id = ${id} ORDER BY sort_order ASC ` // Build the invoice data shape that TemplatePreview expects const invoiceData = { invoice_number: String(invoice.invoice_number), invoice_date: String(invoice.invoice_date), service_date: invoice.service_date ? String(invoice.service_date) : "", description: String(invoice.description), hsn_code: String(invoice.hsn_code || ""), amount_before_tax: Number(invoice.amount_before_tax), cgst_rate: Number(invoice.cgst_rate), sgst_rate: Number(invoice.sgst_rate), cgst_amount: Number(invoice.cgst_amount), sgst_amount: Number(invoice.sgst_amount), total_amount: Number(invoice.total_amount), terms: String(invoice.terms || ""), status: String(invoice.status), client: { name: String(invoice.client?.name || ""), address: String(invoice.client?.address || ""), gstin: String(invoice.client?.gstin || ""), email: String(invoice.client?.email || ""), phone: String(invoice.client?.phone || ""), }, line_items: lineItems.length > 0 ? lineItems.map((li) => ({ description: String(li.description), hsn_code: String(li.hsn_code || ""), qty: Number(li.qty), rate: Number(li.rate), amount: Number(li.amount), })) : [{ description: String(invoice.description), hsn_code: String(invoice.hsn_code || ""), qty: 1, rate: Number(invoice.amount_before_tax), amount: Number(invoice.amount_before_tax), }], } const profileData = profile ? { full_name: profile.full_name || "", email: profile.email || "", phone: profile.phone || "", address: profile.address || "", gstin: profile.gstin || "", pan_no: profile.pan_no || "", bank_name: profile.bank_name || "", account_name: profile.account_name || "", account_number: profile.account_number || "", ifsc_code: profile.ifsc_code || "", } : SAMPLE_PROFILE const amountInWords = numberToWords(invoiceData.total_amount) return ( <> {/* Print toolbar */}

Invoice preview — click Print / Save as PDF

{/* Rendered invoice using saved template */}
{/* Amount in words below the template */}
Total in Words: {amountInWords}
) }

content.text:
All 9 todos completed. Here's what was built:

---

**Invoice Templates** — accessible at `/dashboard/invoices/templates`

**How it works:**
1. Open `Invoice Templates` from the sidebar
2. See 3 starter cards — each shows a live scaled thumbnail of the actual rendered layout
3. Click "Use & Edit" on any card to open the full editor
4. Editor is split: **left controls panel** + **right live A4 preview** (updates instantly as you change anything)

**Style tab — what you can change:**
- Layout: Classic (navy, two-column), Modern (indigo, card-style), Professional (teal, bold header)
- 8 one-click color presets + individual hex pickers for accent, stripe, text, background, and header text
- Heading font + body font (6 options: Inter, Arial, Georgia, Trebuchet, Courier New, Times New Roman)
- Text size: Small / Medium / Large
- Page size: A4 / Letter
- Multi-line items toggle (enables qty + rate columns per row)

**Fields tab — what you can toggle:**
- Sender address, phone, client phone, client GSTIN, HSN/SAC code, service date, CGST+SGST rows, terms, bank details, signature block
- Rename any field label (e.g. "Service Date" → "Period of Service")
- Add unlimited custom fields (label + value) and show/hide each one

**Saving:** "Set as Default" saves and immediately applies the template to all future PDFs. The PDF page at `/dashboard/invoices/[id]/pdf` now loads the saved template automatically.

content.text:
Deployed. Now there are **two quick things you need to do in the Supabase dashboard** (takes about 2 minutes total) to complete both fixes:

---

## 1 — Fix the email redirect (Site URL)

Go to **[Supabase → Authentication → URL Configuration](https://supabase.com/dashboard/project/mrcohlfwsouelynquctd/auth/url-configuration)**

Set these two fields:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Site URL** | `https://v0-design-invoicing.vercel.app` |
| **Redirect URLs** | Add `https://v0-design-invoicing.vercel.app/**` |

Save. From now on, every confirmation email and reset email will point to your live Vercel app instead of localhost.

---

## 2 — Enable Google OAuth

**Step A — Create a Google OAuth App:**
1. Go to [console.cloud.google.com](https://console.cloud.google.com) → Create a project (or pick an existing one)
2. Go to **APIs & Services → Credentials → Create Credentials → OAuth 2.0 Client ID**
3. Application type: **Web application**
4. Under **Authorized redirect URIs**, add exactly:
   ```
   https://mrcohlfwsouelynquctd.supabase.co/auth/v1/callback
   ```
5. Copy the **Client ID** and **Client Secret**

**Step B — Add to Supabase:**
Go to **[Supabase → Authentication → Providers → Google](https://supabase.com/dashboard/project/mrcohlfwsouelynquctd/auth/providers)**

- Toggle **Enable** to on
- Paste your **Client ID** and **Client Secret**
- Save

That's it! The Google button is already live on your login page — once you add the credentials in Supabase, it will work immediately.

---

**What I changed in the code:**
- The signup confirmation email now uses `window.location.origin` dynamically — so it sends `https://v0-design-invoicing.vercel.app/dashboard` when running on Vercel, and `localhost:3000/dashboard` when running locally
- Added a "Continue with Google" button with the real Google logo
- New `/auth/callback` route that handles the OAuth redirect from Google
- New `/reset-password` page where users land after clicking the reset link

**For testing (right now):** Google OAuth apps work immediately in **testing mode**. You just need to add your own email as a "Test User" in Google Cloud Console → OAuth Consent Screen → Test Users. You'll see a warning screen that says "This app isn't verified" but you can click through and it works fine.

**For production (public users):** If you want *any* Google account to log in without seeing a warning, Google requires you to submit the app for verification. That review can take a few days. But you can keep adding test users manually while you wait.

**So the flow is:**

1. Create the OAuth credentials in Google Console (Step 2 from above) — takes 2 minutes
2. Add your email under **OAuth Consent Screen → Test Users**
3. Paste credentials into Supabase (Step 3) — takes 1 minute

The user is asking about whether they need to wait for the Google OAuth app to be reviewed/approved before they can use it.

For Google OAuth apps, there's a distinction:
1. **During development/testing** - You can add up to 100 test users and use the app immediately without going through Google's verification process. The app will show a warning screen ("This app isn't verified") but it works.
2. **For production** - If your app requests sensitive scopes or you want to allow anyone to sign in (not just test users), you need to go through Google's verification process, which can take time.

For our use case (basic login with Google, just asking for email/profile), the app can be used in **testing mode immediately** — you just need to add the user's email as a test user in Google Console.

Found both problems:
1. The middleware checks for Supabase cookies, but `supabase-js` stores sessions in **localStorage** (browser only) — the server-side middleware can't read it
2. The dashboard layout still uses the old Stack Auth `checkAuth()`

The proper fix is to install `@supabase/ssr` which gives Supabase cookie-based sessions that both client AND server can read:

I can see two issues:

1. `DashboardRootLayout` uses `checkAuth()` which is the OLD Stack Auth system. It tries to use Stack Auth, and if `NEXT_PUBLIC_STACK_PROJECT_ID` isn't set, it returns `null` (allows through). But if it IS set... wait, looking at the code: if `!projectId`, it returns null (passes through). So if Stack Auth env var is not set, it passes through. Let me check if this var is set.

2. The middleware checks for session cookies with specific cookie names. Supabase stores sessions differently:
   - In older supabase-js v2, it uses `sb-<ref>-auth-token` cookie format
   - But the browser client from `@supabase/supabase-js` actually stores sessions in localStorage by default, not cookies
   - The middleware is looking for cookies that don't exist, finds nothing, and redirects to login
   - Meanwhile the dashboard layout uses Stack Auth which passes through if not configured

So the real disconnect is that the middleware expects cookies but the Supabase client is storing everything in localStorage.

I have a few paths forward: either switch to `@supabase/ssr` for proper cookie-based sessions, remove the middleware check entirely and handle auth client-side in the layout, or configure the browser client to also set cookies. The simplest approach without adding more dependencies would be to either skip the middleware auth check for dashboard routes or refactor the dashboard layout to properly read the server-side session.

Fix session persistence: migrate to @supabase/ssr cookie-based auth

Previously supabase-js stored sessions only in localStorage (browser),
so the server-side middleware could not read them, causing a redirect
loop after OAuth/Google sign-in.

Changes:
- Install @supabase/ssr for cookie-based session storage
- Split auth helpers: supabase-auth.ts (browser) + supabase-server.ts (server)
- Rewrite middleware.ts to use @supabase/ssr createServerClient with
  proper cookie read/write so session survives server-side checks
- Replace old Stack Auth checkAuth() in dashboard layout with
  Supabase getUser() check via the new server component client
EOF
)" && git push origin main 2>&1 && vercel --prod 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Deployed. Here's what was wrong and what's fixed:

**Root cause:** `supabase-js` stores sessions in the browser's **localStorage** by default. But the middleware and server components run on the server — they can't read localStorage. So every request looked "unauthenticated" to the server, redirecting you back to login.

**Fix:** Switched to `@supabase/ssr` which stores the session in **cookies** instead. Cookies travel with every request, so both the browser client AND the server middleware can read the same session.

**What changed:**
- `lib/supabase-auth.ts` — browser client now uses `@supabase/ssr` (cookies)
- `lib/supabase-server.ts` — new server-only client for layouts/route handlers
- `middleware.ts` — reads the session from cookies using `@supabase/ssr`
- `dashboard/layout.tsx` — replaced old Stack Auth check with Supabase `getUser()`

Try signing in with Google now — it should land on the dashboard and stay there.

content.text:
The issue is that the client-side `exchangeCodeForSession` can fail silently. The recommended approach is a **server-side route handler** for the callback. Let me switch to that:

Good - I can see that `createBrowserClient` uses cookies for storage (via `createStorageFromOptions`). It also uses `flowType: "pkce"`. This is correct.

Now I notice something important: `cachedBrowserClient` - it's a singleton. This means that when `createBrowserClient()` is called in the login page AND in the callback page, they might get the same client instance. This should be fine.

Now let me think about the actual issue again. The user says they're being redirected back to the signup/login page after Google sign-in.

The flow is:
1. Click "Continue with Google" on login page


2. `signInWithOAuth` initiates the PKCE flow, storing a code verifier in a cookie
3. User gets redirected to Google's authorization page
4. Google redirects back to `/auth/callback` with the authorization code and state
5. The callback exchanges the code for a session using the stored code verifier
6. Session gets saved to cookies and `router.replace("/dashboard")` redirects to the dashboard
7. The middleware should detect the session cookie and allow access

But somehow the user is still ending up back at the login page, which suggests either the session isn't being stored properly or the middleware isn't finding it.

I'm wondering if the cached browser client is causing issues - when both the login and callback pages call `createBrowserClient()`, they should get the same cached instance, but maybe there's something about how the session is being shared between them. More importantly, I need to verify whether `getUser()` in the middleware actually works with just the access token cookie, or if it needs both the access and refresh tokens from the session.

Let me trace through what's happening: after `exchangeCodeForSession` completes, I should check what cookies are actually being set in the browser. The real problem could be that the PKCE code verifier from the initial OAuth sign-in isn't available when the callback tries to exchange the code, or the session cookies aren't making it to the middleware, or the `getUser()` call itself is failing silently.

I think a better approach is to move this to a server-side route handler instead of a client component. Since `@supabase/ssr` stores the PKCE verifier in cookies, a server route can read those cookies and handle the exchange directly, which avoids the complexity of managing state across client-side operations.
Now delete the client-side page (can't have both for the same path):
Also show any OAuth error on the login page and fix the login page to handle the `error` search param:

Fix OAuth flow: switch to server-side callback route

Client-side exchangeCodeForSession was failing silently — the session
was not making it into cookies before the middleware ran.

/auth/callback/route.ts (server-side GET handler). The server route:

- creates a Supabase SSR client with cookie read/write

)" && git push origin main 2>&1 && vercel --prod 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Deployed. Here's exactly what changed and why it should work now:

**What was wrong:** The client-side callback page called `exchangeCodeForSession` using JavaScript in the browser. If that call had any error (wrong code, already used code, network hiccup), it failed silently and still redirected to `/dashboard` — where the middleware saw no session and bounced you back to login.

**What's fixed now:** The `/auth/callback` is now a **server-side route**. When Google redirects back, the server:
1. Reads the code from the URL
2. Reads the PKCE code verifier from the cookie (set when you first clicked "Continue with Google")
3. Exchanges the code for a session and writes the session cookie
4. Then redirects to `/dashboard` — with the session cookie already set

If anything fails, you'll now see a proper error message on the login page instead of a silent loop.

Try signing in with Google again — it should go straight to the dashboard this time.
